FT_IC1.evidence: Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study, n=9,208, 21 U.S. recruitment sites, baseline ages 9-10, ABCD 4.0 release; Methods p.391: "The current study analyzed prospective data from the ABCD study...11,875 children recruited from 21 sites around the United States."

FT_IC5.evidence: Original prospective cohort analysis: logistic regression of baseline screen time (exposure) on OCD at 2-year follow-up (outcome), three model specifications reported in Table 3.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Journal of Adolescent Health 72 (2023) 390-396; no indication of duplicate.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 hit: Table 3 (Model 3) reports adjusted OR for "Social networking" = 1.07 (95% CI 0.83-1.39) and for "Texting" = 1.20 (95% CI 1.00-1.44) paired with OCD (individual-trait psychiatric outcome). More clearly, "Videos (YouTube)" aOR 1.11 (p=.038) and "Video games" aOR 1.15 (p=.015) also reported as named modalities. The exposure measure includes "social media [e.g., Facebook, Instagram, Twitter]" as a separately-measured modality with its own coefficient in Table 3 (social networking aOR reported for all three models), meeting the phone/SM-specific label requirement paired with OCD as individual-trait outcome.

FT_IC3.evidence: Table 3 reports adjusted ORs with 95% CIs and p-values for all screen modalities predicting OCD at 2-year follow-up across three models.

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric results reported: e.g., total screen time aOR 1.05 (95% CI 1.01-1.09, p=.012); video games aOR 1.15 (95% CI 1.03-1.28, p=.015); videos aOR 1.11 (95% CI 1.01-1.23, p=.038) in Model 3.

FT_EC7_reason: Screen time (including social media) is the IV (exposure) and OCD is the DV (outcome); SM appears as IV, so condition (b) of EC7 is not met.
notes: Prospective cohort, baseline 2016-2018, 2-year follow-up 2018-2020. Six screen modalities measured separately including social media. Primary headline finding is video games and watching videos prospectively associated with OCD; social media aOR non-significant but coefficient reported.

FT_IC1.evidence: Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study, Year 2 data (2018-2020), n=10,280, 21 U.S. research sites; Methods: "The Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study is a longitudinal study that started following a cohort of 11,878 9-10-years-olds from 21 research sites within the U.S."

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical cross-sectional analysis: modified Poisson regression of bedtime screen behaviors (exposures) on sleep outcomes (trouble falling/staying asleep; overall sleep disturbance), reported in Table 3.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Sleep Health: Journal of the National Sleep Foundation 9 (2023) 497-502; no indication of duplicate.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 hit: Table 3 reports adjusted risk ratios for qualifying phone/SM-specific variables paired with sleep (individual-trait health outcome): "Talk on the phone or text" ARR 1.16 (95% CI 1.09-1.23, p<.001) for trouble falling/staying asleep and ARR 1.09 (95% CI 1.04-1.13, p<.001) for sleep disturbance; "Spend time online on social media (eg, Facebook)" ARR 1.16 (95% CI 1.10-1.23, p<.001) for trouble falling/staying asleep; "Leave the ringer on" (phone behavior) ARR 1.23 (95% CI 1.03-1.46, p=.022). These are named phone/SM-specific labels with their own coefficients.

FT_IC3.evidence: Table 3 reports adjusted risk ratios with 95% CIs and p-values for all bedtime screen behaviors predicting two sleep outcomes.

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric results reported: e.g., social media ARR 1.16 (95% CI 1.10-1.23) for trouble sleeping; talk/text ARR 1.16 (95% CI 1.09-1.23); phone ringer-on ARR 1.23 (95% CI 1.03-1.46).

FT_EC7_reason: Cross-sectional design; FT-EC7 requires longitudinal design as condition (a), which is not met here.
notes: Cross-sectional, Year 2 ABCD data. Screen use around bedtime (9-item measure) as exposure; sleep disturbance outcomes. Multiple qualifying phone/SM-specific variables (texting/phone, social media, phone ringer) each have own ARR coefficients paired with sleep outcomes.

FT_IC1.evidence: Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study, N=11,875, 21 U.S. sites, ABCD 3.0 release, baseline 2016-2018 (ages 9-10), 1-year follow-up 2017-2019; Methods: "We analyzed prospective data from the ABCD Study, a longitudinal study of brain development and adolescent health in 11,875 children recruited from 21 sites around the United States (U.S.)."

FT_IC5.evidence: Original prospective cohort analysis: modified Poisson regression of baseline screen time (total and by modality) on conduct disorder and oppositional defiant disorder at 1-year follow-up, reported in Tables 2, 3, and 4.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Journal of Child Psychology and Psychiatry 64(1):125-135 (2023); no indication of duplicate.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 hit: Table 2 reports adjusted PR for "Social networking" = 1.62 (95% CI 1.39-1.87, p<.001) predicting conduct disorder (individual-trait behavioral health outcome). Table 3 reports adjusted PR for "Video chat" = 1.21 (95% CI 1.06-1.37, p<.001) and "Texting" = 1.19 (95% CI 1.07-1.33, p<.001) predicting oppositional defiant disorder. Social networking (social media), texting, and video chat are qualifying phone/SM-specific variables with their own coefficients paired with individual-trait psychiatric outcomes.

FT_IC3.evidence: Tables 2 and 3 report unadjusted and adjusted prevalence ratios with 95% CIs for all screen modalities predicting conduct disorder and oppositional defiant disorder at 1-year follow-up.

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric results include: social networking PR 1.62 (95% CI 1.39-1.87) for conduct disorder; video chat PR 1.21 (95% CI 1.06-1.37) and texting PR 1.19 (95% CI 1.07-1.33) for ODD; total screen time PR 1.07 (95% CI 1.03-1.11) for conduct disorder.

FT_EC7_reason: Screen time (including social media, texting, video chat) is the IV (exposure) and conduct/ODD disorders are the DVs (outcomes); SM appears as IV, so condition (b) of EC7 is not met.
notes: Prospective cohort, ABCD 3.0 release. Six screen modalities measured separately at baseline. Social media (social networking) is the strongest predictor of conduct disorder (PR 1.62); texting and video chat predict ODD. All three papers in this batch are by Nagata et al. and use overlapping ABCD data releases but examine distinct outcomes (OCD, sleep, conduct/ODD) and are separate original analyses.
